data.title: Boston Scientific: Continuing Its M&A Playbook, As Organic Achievements Impress
data.description:
At the start of 2024, I concluded that continued M&A efforts were driving growth in the case of Boston Scientific (NYSE:BSX) an observation that remains very true today.

At the time, shares hit fresh all-time highs at levels near the $60 mark, as M&A was accompanied by real mid- to high-single digit organic growth, impressive given the M&A strategy employed as well.

In the meantime, the company stuck to posting impressive organic growth rates, in fact, organic growth has only accelerated, while it continues to play its M&A playbook. In less than two years' time, revenues are up some 40%, which, combined with margin expansion, means that almost all the impressive share price gains have been backed up by real earnings growth.

This makes me comforted on the one hand, yet a 3% earnings yield feels a bit low in this interest rate environment, although the performance remains too impressive to disregard the shares here.

Shedding Some Perspective

In the decade leading up to 2024, Boston Scientific doubled sales from $7 billion to $14 billion, although the company incurred some 10% dilution over this period of time. M&A and organic growth grew the business larger in terms of size, but also more diversified across categories.

While this was to be applauded, I had not forgotten about the past, as a $27 billion deal for Guidant caused shares to rise to a high (at the time) around $40 per share. After that, the company saw its shares being pressured, with shares for the first time revisiting those levels in 2019.

Given that past episode and the fundamentals, I was leaning cautious. After all, the company was on track to post earnings around $2 per share for all of 2023, as a $58 stock was valued at a 29 times earnings multiple. With nearly 1.5 billion shares trading at $58 and factoring in $8 billion in net debt, the $93 billion enterprise valuation valued the business at 6.5 times sales of $14 billion, and at a 30 earnings multiple, both being rather demanding multiples.

All this was about to change as the business announced a $3.4 billion deal to acquire medical device company Axonics, adding $366 million in revenues for the treatment of overactive bladder and fecal incontinence. In all, I ended up concluding that Boston deserved more credit over time, yet that overall expectations were too high for me to get involved with the shares here.

Strong Momentum

In the near two years since the start of 2024, shares of Boston Scientific have seen gains of more than 60%, now trading at the $103 mark, down from an absolute high of $109 per share.

Forwarding to February of this year, Boston Scientific reported a near 18% increase in full year sales to $16.7 billion, almost all driven by organic growth. This was driven by a 22% organic increase in cardiovascular sales to $10.8 billion, with a near $6 billion MedSurg business posting sales up 10% on the year before. The strong momentum drove earnings growth in a major way as well, with adjusted earnings up from $2.05 per share in 2023 to $2.51 per share in 2024.

The company guided for a 10-12% increase in organic sales growth, with reported sales growth seen 2.5% higher, driving earnings per share, which were seen moving up towards $2.80-$2.87 per share.

Momentum was only accelerating as the company continued to pursue M&A this year. This includes the purchase of a remaining 74% stake in Bolt Medical at the start of the year. This, however, is another bolt-on deal, with the remaining shares valued at $443 million, excluding $221 million in regulatory milestones payable.

A similar-sized deal followed in March, when the company announced the purchase of a remaining 90% stake in SoniVie, with the price for the remaining shares pegged at $380 million, excluding a potential $180 million regulatory milestone payment due.

Aided by continued M&A efforts, the company reported a 21% increase in first quarter sales, again largely driven by organic growth. As a result, the company hiked the full year organic growth guidance by two points to 12-14%, with adjusted earnings seen between $2.87 and $2.94 per share.

Second quarter sales grew by nearly 23%, with revenues now comfortably trending at $20 billion per annum, as full year earnings are now seen at a midpoint of $2.97 per share.

Third quarter sales grew by another 20%, with organic growth still exceeding a healthy 15%. Continued momentum across the board means that the midpoint of the full year earnings guidance has been hiked further to $3.03 per share. The very strong organic performance has been driven by strong Cardiovascular results, certainly in the Watchman and Electrophysiology segments. With the share count coming in just shy of 1.5 billion shares, the market value of the firm has risen to $154 billion.

Net debt is reported at $11.0 billion as of the second quarter (with the third quarter 8-K filing not yet made), yet with adjusted EBITDA trending comfortably above $5 billion, leverage ratios are very reasonable at just over 2 times. Trading around the $103 mark, the $165 billion enterprise valuation pushed up the valuation to 8 times sales, and while margins have improved, earnings multiples have expanded to 34 times currently, however for good reasons, that of near continued >15% organic growth rates!

More M&A

In a typical deal for Boston Scientific, the latest acquisition was announced in October. The company reached a $533 million cash deal to acquire the remaining equity stake in Nalu Medical, after the company had been an investor in the business since 2017.

The deal is designed to expand its ability to develop and commercialize innovative and minimally invasive solutions for patients with chronic pain. With a $60 million revenue contribution, the deal is not cheap at 10 times sales (with 100% of the equity valued at $600 million), but so is Boston itself.

The deal is seen as boosting sales by 0.3%, adding to the growth profile, with 2026 growth rates seen in excess of 25%!

A Final Thought

The truth is that a 3% earnings yield normally does not look that enticing, certainly not in this interest rate environment. However, Boston Scientific is clearly setting itself apart from its peers, posting 20% growth rates. Moreover, the observation is that these are largely organic growth achievements, although M&A plays a role in this as well.

After all, bolt-on M&A efforts only count for the first year as inorganic growth, but when they are scaled up within Boston Scientific thereafter, their growth from year two counts as organic growth. Certainly there is an element of that, although those real organic growth achievements are very impressive as well.

In between all this, I am hard-pressed to chase the shares here, although that relative appeal seems on the rise here, with shares trading largely flattish so far in 2025. This makes me interested to watch continued M&A moves, with a real intent of buying even relatively modest dips here.

data.title: Evolution AB: I'm Not Giving Up Yet, But My Patience Is Running Out
data.description:
Yesterday, Evolution AB (publ) (OTCPK:EVVTY) released its Q3 2025 report and the market reaction was quite negative. The stock dropped 8%, and I was personally disappointed as well by this quarter. Net sales decreased 2.40%, cybersecurity remains a major issue in Asia, and after the European ring-fencing, the regulatory risk is now spreading towards all of Asia (India, Philippines above all).

This is getting frustrating, as the improvements we saw in Q2 2025 have been totally wiped out. CEO Martin Carlesund is confident about a recovery, but he didn’t provide any guidance, so we have no clue how long these problems will continue.

Let’s see in detail what this Q3 2025 is all about.

A big problem to solve

The situation in Asia is getting serious, and this is affecting the whole company: this is the root of almost all problems.

Asia accounts for 37% of total revenues and this makes it the most important geographical region. In Q3 2025, revenues declined 6.50% YoY and 9.60% QoQ, a very disappointing performance.

During the last quarter we saw that revenues started growing once again, so investors (including me) were optimistic about a potential recovery; but now all the progress has been wiped out. Cybersecurity concerns are still there, and on top of this, new regulatory problems arose.

So the situation on the market in Asia, is a lot of different countries. We point out, Philippines is very volatile right now. There's things happening in India, but there's also other countries that are fluctuating and things are happening there as well. India is a large country. There are regions that have portions regulated when it comes to sports. There's a desire in some regions to regulate. Now there are suggestions on a sort of federal level to take a few steps towards blocking online gaming. These types of movements we often see, when there is talks and happenings about regulation, it opens up for different routes forward. It could go into regulation, it could settle down or it could go to somewhere else. We can't speculate on that. We just see that it's affecting us to a certain level right now.

CEO Martin Carlesund.

India and the Philippines are going through a period of new regulations toward online gaming, and we can’t know how all this will evolve. The management didn’t elaborate further on the matter, they only specified that India and the Philippines are not the only regions involved in this regulatory process. This definitely doesn’t bode well.

About cybersecurity issues, this theme has been going on for several quarters and now it is getting frustrating. The Q2 2025 results offered a glimmer of hope, but this Q3 2025 shows an entirely different picture.

I understand that it causes some frustration, especially since we actually saw some signs of improvement in the second quarter. And believe me, I'm frustrated, too. I can only repeat that it requires patience while being a top priority.

CEO Martin Carlesund.

Even Martin Carlesund seems quite nervous about it, and as a shareholder, I am getting worried about this cybersecurity issue. At first, I thought it was a momentary problem, and the Q2 2025 proved that I was right, but now my view is changing. What if this problem is permanent in Asia?

During the conference call, management stated that what is happening in Asia is something that can’t be solved with money. Evolution is a cash machine with no debt, so it could afford the best cybersecurity products on the market, but this seems not to be enough. Management urges patience, but mine is gradually running out. I would like to see more details on how the company is managing this issue as this reticence is not helping. The only thing that we do know is that there won’t be any improvement over the next quarter.

When it comes to Asia, to address this problem, it's technically difficult. It's very advanced, and we're doing it. And we're tuning and we're finding. My belief is over time, that we will find the right balance and the right solutions and continuously enhance and protect our product to make it even better. So in the longer perspective, I look at a very good situation in the Asian market. Now I don't have any -- I can't share any sort of time frame on that right now.

CEO Martin Carlesund.

As time goes by, I am getting less optimistic about Asia as a whole. It represents 37% of total revenue (last year 39%), but at this rate it will become a minor segment; this shrinking process will necessarily hurt the overall revenue growth of the next years. However, out of this mess we can still find some positive news:

The first one is that Evolution is improving its entire cybersecurity system. The investments made in Asia are useful to fight the same problems all over the world. To some extent, this further improves Evolution’s competitive advantage.

Second, since Asia is the region where Evolution gets the most of its unregulated revenue, the company is improving its exposure to regulated revenue. The latter are now 46% of the total, a year ago just 39%.

Overall, the situation in Asia remains very challenging, and I wonder whether this issue is momentary or not. Some months ago, I was sure about the first one.

Why I am still optimistic

At this point you might think that my strong buy rating makes no sense, after all the most important region where revenue comes from is declining by double digits. Well, we shouldn’t overlook what is happening in Asia, but Evolution is not just that.

North America and Latin America both experienced an expansion: revenue grew 14.50% and 6.40% YoY, respectively. The company is planning to open a second studio in Michigan by mid-2026, and now Crazy Time (one of the most successful live-casino games) is available in Connecticut. About the Galaxy Gaming acquisition, the management seems quite certain to get the regulatory approval by year-end. Regarding Latin America, the new studio in São Paulo is performing as expected and it will probably be expanded.

Let’s now turn to Europe, a region that is pretty close to Asia in terms of revenue.

Europe has the potential to become the most important region over the next quarters as regulatory risk seems behind us. The QoQ revenue growth is little but gives rise to hope that the bottom was reached last quarter.

We saw the full effect of our protective ring-fencing measures in the second quarter, and this provided a new foundation for growth. Worth mentioning is also that we got recognition for our ring fencing from one of the largest regulators in Europe, where we were pointed out as one of the best B2B suppliers. I'm happy with the progress in Europe in this quarter.

CEO Martin Carlesund.

As you can see, if we exclude Asia from the equation, the picture is not that bad. Even profitability still looks impressive.

The net profit margin is 50%, something that only a few companies in the world can achieve. Of course, revenue growth is disappointing, but I think it is worth waiting a few more quarters before throwing in the towel. If the profitability remains so high, it means that this company still has its competitive advantage, and the underlying business is extremely interesting.

To make you understand how undervalued this company potentially is, I want to make an absurd assumption: what if Evolution doesn’t grow over the next 10 years? Would it still be worth it?

Well, it could still deliver a 12% annual return over the next 10 years. This shows how much negativity currently surrounds this company. If it gets back to growing double digits (10%), the 10Y CAGR on your investment spikes to 20%, doubling the long-term stock market average return. I wouldn’t rule out this possibility, that’s why I didn’t sell a single share.

Consider that I am not factoring in any share buybacks, even though the company is committed to doing so.

In Q3 2025, there were share repurchases of €187 million; €406.50 million YTD. Instead of issuing the large dividend (4.40% yield), I would appreciate a further focus on buyback, but this probably won’t happen.

Conclusion

Evolution is facing a very challenging phase where its most important segment doesn’t grow anymore. We still don’t see the light at the end of the tunnel, but at least Europe and America are showing encouraging signs.

As stated by the CEO Martin Carlesund, more patience is needed to see a full recovery, but it probably won’t be in Q4 2025. I would like more details on the matter, but I remain optimistic thanks to the higher profitability and the DCF valuation.

The market's vision is blurred by negativity, but we know that this is the moment where you get a bargain. Of course, investing in Evolution provides lots of risks as we have seen, but I want to give it more time. I won’t buy more shares just because I already invested too much in it: diversification matters.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Sanctions Rattle The Oil Market
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy

The US sanctions two key Russian oil producers

Oil prices received a jolt higher this week after the Trump administration announced sanctions on Russian oil producers, Rosneft and Lukoil. These are the first direct sanctions that the administration has placed on Russia during President Trump’s second term. The announcement comes after plans for a summit between President Trump and President Putin in Budapest fell through, leaving Trump increasingly frustrated at the lack of progress in Russia and Ukraine moving towards a peace deal.

Rosneft and Lukoil produce more than a combined 5m b/d of crude oil, which is in the region of 50% of total Russian oil production and so these sanctions have the potential to be very disruptive to the oil market.

These are not the first Russian oil producers to be sanctioned by the US. In January, ahead of President Biden leaving the White House, sanctions were placed on Gazprom Neft and Surgutneftegas, along with a large portion of Russia’s shadow tanker fleet. These sanctions also led to initial concerns over supply disruptions; however, they ultimately had little impact on Russian oil flows.

These latest sanctions are set to come into full effect on 21 November 2025, allowing a wind-down of transactions with both Rosneft and Lukoil, as well as with any subsidiaries in which they hold 50% or more ownership.

The risk of buyers being penalised for transacting with Rosneft and Lukoil is a concern for the market and threatens a large portion of Russian oil supply.

Why has the Trump administration taken this action now?

Apart from President Trump growing increasingly frustrated with Putin and the lack of progress toward a peace deal, the state of the oil market also provides the US with the opportunity to take a more aggressive stance against Russia. Prior to the announcement of sanctions, the market was set for a significant surplus through the remainder of this year and 2026, which has weighed heavily on oil prices, with Brent trading down towards US$60/bbl. Lower oil prices create the opportunity to impose sanctions, whilst expectations of abundant supply also provide comfort that the market can manage potential losses in Russian oil supply.

What does this mean for Russian oil flows?

For the oil market, the most important point is how impactful these sanctions will be on Russian oil flows. This includes both crude oil and refined products. Russia exports a little more than 4.5m b/d of crude oil and in the region of 2.5m b/d of refined products, of which around 1m b/d is diesel. This helps explain the strength seen in the ICE gasoil crack over the week, amid concerns of further tightness in the middle distillate market.

Looking at the crude oil market, there are clear signs that buyers are looking for alternative grades, particularly from the Middle East. This is evident when looking at the Brent-Dubai spread, which has collapsed since the sanctions' announcement (driven by strength in the Dubai benchmark).

China is the largest buyer of Russian crude oil, taking 2m b/d so far this year. This will include both volumes via pipeline as well as by vessel. Meanwhile, flows to India have averaged a little more than 1.5m b/d so far this year. Other buyers include Turkey, which has taken roughly 290k b/d so far this year, while Hungary and Slovakia, which received an exemption from the EU ban on Russian oil, source almost all their needs from Russia via the Druzhba pipeline.

For the oil market, most focus will be on the sizeable volumes going into China and India. For India, there are already reports that Indian refiners are looking for alternative grades from the Middle East. Prior to these sanctions, India was facing secondary tariffs due to its purchases of Russian oil. Therefore, there had been talk already that Russian flows to India would fall dramatically.

For China, there have also been reports that state refiners there have backed out of some oil purchases following the sanction announcement. However, we believe that Russian oil flows to the country will likely remain more resilient. In fact, there is potential for volumes to pick up, particularly if India stops buying, which could see China picking up Urals at an even larger discount.

We could also see smaller buyers, such as Hungary, Slovakia and Turkey, seek alternative supply. However, it would not be surprising if Hungary and Slovakia seek an exemption from the US, citing difficulties in obtaining alternative supplies, as both countries are landlocked.

There will be an element of scepticism over how effective these sanctions will be. Russia has demonstrated its ability to keep oil flows going despite sanctions and embargoes since 2022. The price action that we have seen since the announcement appears to reflect these doubts, with Brent up a little more than 5% on the day sanctions were issued. Clearly, one would expect much more of a move if the belief was that a meaningful amount of Russian supply would be lost.

Does this dramatically shift the outlook for the oil market?

There is still plenty of uncertainty over how much Russian oil supply will be disrupted by these latest sanctions, and as a result, there is significant uncertainty about what this means for the oil balance and prices.

Prior to these sanctions, we held a bearish view on the market, expecting Brent to average $57/bbl over 2026, with prices weighed down by the scale of the surplus set to hit the market. We are holding back from making any revisions to our forecasts until there is more clarity on exactly what the impact of these sanctions will be on oil flows.

However, the shift seen from the Trump administration and the risk of further sanctions suggest that the floor for the market could end up being somewhat higher.

In addition, if we start seeing Russian flows becoming increasingly disrupted, we could see a scenario where Brent trades up to the $70-75/bbl range, assuming we lose in the region of 1.5m b/d of Russian supply (essentially all supply to India).

Much will also depend on how other OPEC+ members respond. The group has brought a significant amount of supply back onto the market since April this year, and any supply losses from Russia leave room for the group to tap further into its spare production capacity, helping to cap the upside in prices.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Intel: Momentum Keeps On Building Based On The Turnaround (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) has been one of the most closely followed earnings releases by investors following the slew of investments made in the company. This has pushed the company’s stock up by more than 90%, without accounting for the current pre-market rise. Following my last analysis on the company back in September, I want to see how the story might have changed with the recent earnings.

To remind readers, Intel has been undergoing a major shift since Lip-Bu Tan took over as CEO, focusing on profitability and key partnerships to help the company go back to the forefront of innovation, mainly in its AI strategy.

Current Dynamics

Intel just announced a strong double beat, further fueling its upward momentum. Q3 Non-GAAP EPS came in at 23 cents, a beat of 22 cents, while revenue reached $13.7B, up 3.2% Y/Y and a beat of over $560MM. What a difference a year makes, especially when looking at net income that came in at around $4.1B, while a year prior, it was a loss of more than $16.6B. I was particularly interested in the performance of its Client Computing Group, which is basically what helps to finance the core of Intel’s operation. The segment was up 5% Y/Y, demonstrating how the cycle is turning in favor of Intel thanks to the PC refresh to Windows 11. I have to say that I’ve rarely seen such a company turn from a laggard with everything against it to all positives. Though Intel’s foundry is currently operating at a loss, which I expected, it is narrowing, and again this is a long-term play, a bet on American manufacturing that has been secured by all the funding that Intel has recently received.

Looking ahead, Intel is being cautiously optimistic, which is generally a good sign for shareholders, as its Q4 guidance is perceived to be conservative. Intel is expecting revenue to come in the range of $12.8B and $13.8, while adjusted non-GAAP EPS is set to come in at 8 cents a share. Now it is important to note that the guidance excludes revenue from Altera, which Intel just sold a majority stake in. This should remove up to $500MM in revenue from the Q4 forecast.

On the broader industry outlook, there has been another tailwind for Intel-based products with management noting that the current demand is outpacing supply for CPUs and that should persist into 2026. This means the market is currently facing supply shortages, which is particularly good for Intel as its 10nm and 7nm manufacturing processes are in high demand.

I also want to touch on what I believe is probably the most crucial dynamic for Intel right now, technology-wise. Intel’s 18A could become a fundamental shift in chip architecture and has become the cornerstone of the company’s turnaround plan to go back to its roots as the leader in chip innovation. I haven’t delved that much into the process in my previous analyses, and I believe that it is time to shed light on it. 18A stands for 1.8-nanometer class manufacturing process, which should help Intel catch up to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) and Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) manufacturing-wise and even leapfrog them if successful. 18A is set to combine two brand-new techs, PowerVia and RibbonFET, to outpace TSMC’s 2nm node, which does not have backside power delivery. And though this is a massive endeavor, Intel is currently on track in its timeline as 18A has entered production at its new Fab 52 in Chandler, Arizona, with products such as the CPUs Panther Lake for AI PCs that will be widely available starting in January 2026, as well as the new Xeon 6+. Another 18A win, as well as a foundry win, would be the recent announcement that Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) will be using the 18A process to manufacture its next generation of custom AI accelerator, boosting the narrative around Intel gaining back market share.

Overall, I believe that this was a great quarter for Intel, not only for its double beat but more so for the implementation of Lip-Bu Tan’s strategy that is currently massively paying off for shareholders.

Valuation

As the company is currently undergoing its transition towards profitability and strategic partnerships, the current valuation might be slightly skewed by the prior poor results. Though the recent net income figure was largely driven by the sale of stakes in Altera as well as Mobileye Global Inc. (MBLY), which made Intel receive around $5B in cash from these transactions. Intel remains somewhat expensive with its current financials, yet there’s way more optimism now for the company than ever in the company’s recent history. Its current EV/EBITDA of 22.7 is high, though it is certainly well below Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and far higher than TSMC.

It is important to note that the current valuation hinges on the turnaround potential of Intel and, therefore, there is heightened risk in investing in Intel now. Albeit, it is fair to say that the company has one of the biggest advantages available on the market going for it, the government’s backing.

On the growth front, I expressed a lot of optimism about 18A, but Intel will need to be able to convince customers to buy the next generation of chipsets. The company is grappling with a higher selling mix of older products, which is strong for overall profitability, but it leaves us wondering if current customers are enticed by what Intel can offer on the technological front. Especially now that AMD has captured that end of the market with its Ryzen chipsets, which will probably see the same kind of macro tailwinds as Intel with the PC growth and demand.

For profitability, I mentioned how much I was looking at the Client Computing Group, and I was pleased to see that Intel reported a strong operating income of $2.7B on the back of the heightened demand, far higher than the previous quarters. The same trend can be seen for Data Center and AI, which have reached an EBIT of $964MM, the highest since Q1 2022, probably on the back of their struck partnerships with companies like Nvidia.

Still, Intel has a long road ahead to regain the levels of its peers, such as AMD. The foundry business continues to be a loss-making enterprise, but I do not take it against Intel for the time being, as it is what has pushed for the recent investments in the company to make it a viable contender against TSMC. It will require a lot of CapEx ahead but should become a viable option at the technological front for many fabless semiconductors.

You may notice that my valuation segment differs from my usual analysis, but it is mostly because Intel is currently one of those rare companies that is undergoing such a drastic shift in its overall investment thesis that we should look at it outside of simple value-based investing eyes.

Risks

The main risk for Intel right now would be if the current turnaround of the company does not work out long-term, as it is being aggressive in its cost-cutting endeavors, potentially slowing down innovation for the company. It is still facing high challenges despite the recent tailwinds, and a set of a few weak quarters could erode investors’ confidence.

Intel's strategy also heavily relies on the 18A process node and the ability to mass-produce it. If the company falls behind on its strategy, even if it stated that it was on track, this could directly be a major setback as customers are now banking on Intel going back to the top of the technological edge. Furthermore, being at the front of the foundry process could be the main and only selling point for customers to use the fab, as Intel is still a competitor for many of them, while TSMC is not.

Conclusion

With all of that in mind, strategic investments and quarterly earnings, I believe that currently investing in Intel could be interesting for risk-profile investors considering all the momentum that the company has garnered in a relatively short time period. There is a lot of hype and possibilities for everything to go wrong, yet Intel is currently in possibly the most interesting period of its history. I rate Intel as a very small Buy.

data.title: Korea Electric Power: Promising Nuclear Energy Expansion
data.description:
The Korean state owned part-nuclear energy utility Korea Electric Power Corporation (NYSE:KEP), or KEPCO for short, has risen by a massive 120% YTD. This isn't surprising, going by the company's increasing focus on nuclear energy at a time of extreme bullishness around it.

Here I take a closer look at whether this focus can support further price increases in the stock even now. To assess this, I start with a look at South Korea's nuclear energy landscape, followed by KEPCO's advances in the sector and finally on the company's fundamentals, market multiples and outlook for it.

South Korea's nuclear energy focus

To understand KEPCO's nuclear energy business, it's essential to first take a broader look at South Korea's current state of play. As it happens, the country is a key player in global nuclear energy generation as the fifth biggest contributor after the US, France, China and Russia (see chart below).

While coal is still the country's biggest power generating source, with a 33% share as of 2024, nuclear energy comes a close second. The segment contributed to 31% of the total last year, and is expected to increase further.

As per the country's 11th Basic Plan for Long-Term Electricity Supply and Demand [BLPE], which covers the period of 2024 to 2038, polluting fuels like coal and gas are being de-emphasised. On the other hand, nuclear energy's contribution is expected to inch up to ~32% by 2030 and further to 35% by 2038.

The fossil fuel drag for KEPCO

The combination of a phasing out in fossil fuel usage coupled with expansion of nuclear energy capacity is a significant potential positive for KEPCO. For the three years of 2021 to 2023, the company suffered operating losses, which were significantly due to high fuel and purchased power prices.

Even though, as per Korea Times it raised the "average electricity rate by a combined 50 percent on seven occasions since 2022", it wasn't before 2024 that the company reported an operating profit. To cope with the losses, KEPCO took on debt. This is reflected in a ballooning of the net debt-to-assets ratio to 54% by 2023 from 32.4% in 2020.

The company expects to phase out coal completely by 2050 (see slide below), with 10 units already abolished as of 2017. This suggests that over the next years, fluctuations in fossil fuel prices are likely to impact the company's financial fortunes increasingly lesser.

KEPCO's nuclear energy expansion

A key replacement for coal-based energy generation is going to be nuclear energy for KEPCO, though it expects to focus more on LNG as well. The company's subsidiary Korea Hydro and Nuclear Power [KHNP] produces primarily nuclear energy, which already brings in a significant proportion of the total revenues, at 17.7% in Q2 2025. KHNP plans to expand not only domestically but also internationally in the next years. Here are the details of both.

Domestic developments

At the domestic level, the company expects a 1.32x increase in nuclear energy capacity by the end of 2038. This exceeds the total increase of 1.17x expected over this time, though it does need to be mentioned that the increase in LNG capacity is forecast to rise even faster by over 2x. With regards to nuclear, the company plans to add two full size power plants and one small modular reactor [SMR] by 2038.

Growing international operations

The company's nuclear energy business has presence internationally only in the UAE. It supplied four nuclear reactors to the country, which operationalised between 2021 and 2024. There's far more expansion in store, however. Here are the plans by geography:

Europe: KHNP is part of a consortium of companies engaged in the refurbishment of a unit at the Romainian Cernavoda Nuclear Power Plant Site, with the goal to extend the plant's life. The company's contract value for the project is KRW 1.2 trillion (USD 830 million).

It has also finally got the go ahead for a USD 18 billion contract to build two nuclear power plants in Czech Republic, after it got blocked due to legal issues. KHNP also signed MoUs with the governments of Serbia to provide training to its nuclear workforce.

Africa: KHNP won a contract to provide infrastructure support for Egypt's first nuclear plant in 2022, which will be run by Russia's Rosatom and is due to start operations next year. More recently it signed an MoU with Zimbabwe to conduct a preliminary feasibility study for deploying an SMR.

US: While the company hasn't collaborated with the US government yet, in its recent earnings call, it said it was open to doing so. It's worth noting though, that KHNP has already collaborated with the fast reactor developer Oklo (OKLO) for SMR technology development. Considering that Oklo has recently been selected by the Department of Energy for a pilot program to develop nuclear fuel lines and is also developing a microreactor for the US Air Force, KHNP's partnership with it sounds like a step in the right direction.

Supportive fundamentals

Supporting KEPCO's nuclear energy expansion are the company's fundamentals. After seeing a robust ~24% increase in revenues in 2024, the number continued to inch up, albeit at a far lower rate of 5.5% YoY, in H1 2025. This is explained by a slightly smaller unit sale price increase (H1 2025: 5.7%, 2024: 6.6%) and also a marginal decline in sales volumes compared with a 0.7% increase last year.

With continued shrinking expenses, though, the company's margins continue to expand. The operating margin rose to 12.8% in H1 2025 from 8.9% in 2024, while the net margin increased to 7.5% in the latest six months from 4% last year.

A short-term Buy case for KEP

The question now is whether KEP is still a stock to buy for both the short and the long term. First, let's consider the short-term. I assume that both the company's revenue growth and net income margin for the full year 2025 will remain the same as that in H1 2025. This results in a net income of KRW 5.6 trillion (USD 3.89 billion), which results in a forward P/E of just 5.05x.

This makes KEP better placed than the nuclear energy utilities I have covered recently. My estimates accorded the lowest forward P/E to the French nuclear energy giant Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY) at 12.02x. On the other hand is Talen Energy (TLN) was estimated to be at 60.5x then, and with the stock's 6% rise in the interim, the ratio will be slightly higher now. Just going by Engie's valuations suggests that KEP can at least double in just the remainder of the year.

Long-term tip

Even if KEPCO's nuclear energy focus were to provide no financial improvements in the next five years, the stock still looks good. Here's how. The company's revenue CAGR for the past decade is at 5.04% and the net income CAGR is 2.26%. Even if it were to continue seeing this growth in income until 2030, the net income figure would be at USD 4.35 billion. This results in a forward P/E of 4.5x. By comparison, my forecasts put Engie's forward P/E at 6.1x even by 2033.

The risks

If KEPCO's experience of the 2021-23 period is anything to go by however, the stock isn't without its risks. Not as long as thermal power is a key contributor to its revenues. Even if the company expects to phase out coal by 2050, the fact remains that its LNG capacity is expected to double. And LNG was one of the reasons for big expense increases during the years of operating losses.

It's possible that even then fossil fuels would matter less over time, especially as KHNP expands internationally. But over at least the next five years, the risk of fuel price fluctuations sustains. And now, the company's debts are already higher, though, to be fair, they are still quite controlled.

Investor takeaway

On the whole, though, there's more to like about the KEPCO story than not. My coverage of pure nuclear energy stocks for the investing service Green Growth Giants shows that if the present momentum continues for the sector, there's massive potential to be realised. And KEPCO can play a crucial role in that as an already established nuclear energy generating company, which distinguishes it from many others in the sector, that are yet to operationalise.

This is already evident in its growing collaborations on nuclear with the rest of the world, not to mention its planned domestic expansion. It has also demonstrated the ability to bounce back from big losses. This is encouraging, even as there can be fluctuations in earnings again. I'm going with a Buy rating for KEP for both the short and the long term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: QuantumScape: Game-Changing Breakthrough Keeps Me Bullish
data.description:
Updating My Thesis On QS Stock

It turned out to be an extremely successful call when I initiated my coverage of QuantumScape Corporation (NYSE:QS) stock with a Buy rating back in January 2025 and then reaffirmed my bullish stance on the stock in June. For the first half of 2025, QS basically went nowhere, but then it took off when the firm announced its next-gen Cobra manufacturing process entering baseline production - since that announcement, QS stock is up over 158% in just 4 months or so:

The Seeking Alpha momentum factor is going through the roof, and I think this strength may be just the beginning, given the recently announced operational milestones and the raised guidance, which serves as a bullish confirmation for all growth investors. I expect continued strength in the coming month. Even if the valuation looks absurd, it doesn't really matter at this point. It's all about execution now, and with the recent updates that we've gotten from QS and its management team, the execution looks good, and hence the stock should attract new buyers and thus adjust higher eventually. I keep my Buy in place for now.

QuantumScape's Q3 Earnings Review

A few hours ago, as I'm writing these lines, QuantumScape released its Q3 2025 earnings results, showing -$0.18 in GAAP EPS for the quarter (a beat of 2 cents), and also significantly narrowing its full-year 2025 adjusted EBITDA loss guidance to -$252.5 million from -$260 million they guided for previously. While it looks like a statistical error difference, I think the very fact that they guided for a narrower loss was already a big change. At the same time, their OPEX also declined on a YoY basis.

The management basically rolled back on the previous CAPEX outlook for the year: Instead of $45-65 million that they wanted to spend last time, now the management forecasted a new range of $30-40 million, so this is the main reason for the adjusted EBITDA loss guidance reduction.

As a result of efficiency gains and process improvements, including from the Cobra process as well as a change in timing of certain equipment ordering, we revised the range of our full year guidance for CapEx to $30 million to $40 million.

Source: QuantumScape's Q3 earnings call.

As you can see, the reduction in capex was a result of "efficiency gains and process improvements," not a slowdown in their expansion plans, and that's what growth investors really needed to see. QS also introduced a new metric—customer billings—and reported $12.8 million on it (as of the end of Q3), primarily from invoicing Volkswagen's PowerCo under their upgraded joint venture deal. It's not much, I agree, and it's not yet GAAP revenue, but I view it as the first major tangible step towards commercialization and a real proof that their capital-light licensing strategy works.

QS is planning "to move away from providing updates on cash runway, and we'll begin providing updates on customer billings," so its earnings visibility should become better, all else equal. QS isn't just an R&D firm anymore—it's on the cusp of commercialization now. So, I'd expect them to report the first actual sales next year and then dramatic expansion further into 2027 and beyond that.

What I like on the operational front is that QuantumScape started to ship its first Cobra-based QSE-5 B1 samples during the quarter, and they're now on track to launch their highly automated pilot line shortly (the Eagle Line).

Our remaining operational goal for the year is to install higher volume cell production equipment for our highly automated pilot line in San Jose named the Eagle Line. Equipment for certain key assembly steps has already been installed on the Eagle Line, and this goal remains on track.

Source: QuantumScape's Q3 earnings call.

After the unveiling of the Ducati V21L race motorcycle, a collaboration with Ducati, Audi, and PowerCo (powered by QuantumScape's technology), I think the company basically proved that its tech is viable in real-world applications, and thus, we can expect more contracts signed (or at least new partnerships) in the coming quarters.

One of the most important stories of the quarter is the extension of the firm's cash runway by a full year. QS ended the quarter with $1.0 billion in short-term liquidity, and it effectively shifted the runway from 2028 to the end of 2029. With the new partnerships between Murata Manufacturing, Corning, and QS for ceramic separator production, that amount of cash became a justification for what my friend Danil Sereda wrote in his upgrade about 2 weeks ago, calling it a "de-risked business model."

From what I see, QS's prospects have only become brighter now, and the Q3 earnings release has only strengthened and justified the rally that took place since mid-2025.

QuantumScape's Valuation Update

A lot here depends on the forward-looking earnings estimates. When I wrote my previous article on QS, the market was projecting ~$4.54 million in sales for FY2026, and now I see $5.95 million (it's a growth of 31% in less than 5 months). Back in 2022, the market was pricing in ~$35 million in sales for FY2026, and then the analysts started to delay their expectations following the lagging operational performance. Things started to change only recently:

Now we see that the FY2028 sales projection has moved up dramatically, almost doubling in size for the past 3 months. I think what we're looking at right now is lagging the actual changes in earnings revisions that may come next because it's only a few hours after the company reported for its Q3. Assumingly, not all the analysts covering the stock managed to revise their models with fresher, more bullish inputs.

I assume that for the fiscal FY2030, QS will be able to have ~$2.19 billion in total sales—it's the figure the consensus had expected in late 2024. If so, then assuming that QS trades at 10x sales in 5 years, which isn't too much for a growth stock, the implied market cap can arrive at $21.9 billion in the next 4-5 years.

This is my long-term target, which keeps me bullish on QS stock.

Risks And Final Words

As I noted last time, most of my reasoning points are actually based on assumptions rather than on actual data from the firm. There's little QS can brag about in terms of financials, other than its improved balance sheet in Q3. They're going to stay in deep operating losses for the foreseeable future. The main risk to my bullish stance remains: QuantumScape can potentially fail at flawless manufacturing of the pistons, the crankshaft, and all the other components of Cobra, and further integration of the Cobra-made separators into a full cell assembly line, although there has been some success in recent months as they reported. The very fact that they've invented a revolutionary engine (the core battery tech) and that Cobra seems to be the breakthrough process for mass-producing the engine block is great, but there's so much that needs to be done now.

Despite the risks, I think that QS still deserves a Buy rating. In my view, the market's current sales expectation for 2027 and beyond that still looks too pessimistic. The progress QS is making and the partnerships it has signed in recent months make me think that the firm can deliver on the late 2024 consensus estimate regarding its 2030 sales. If I'm right on this assumption, then QS can adjust much higher than what it's trading at right now. I'm reiterating my Buy on QS today.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Merck KGaA: An AI-Resistant Pharma Play
data.description:
So, in this article, I'm going to be presenting you with one of my current, favorite, undervalued pharma plays (there are a few, but this and an alternative that I will also showcase later in the article are my definite favorites). Merck KGaA (OTCPK:MKGAF) is a German company that I've covered previously in a number of articles. It went from uninteresting and overvalued a number of years ago, to fair value and slightly undervalued, to the current state that I consider to be deep undervaluation.

Merck KGaA has another advantage that I consider to be very interesting in the larger picture. Namely, that I consider the operations and the company to be very resistant to trends in AI, meaning that I don't consider the company's profits and revenues to be that impacted by the application of AI.

I will, of course, justify this in the article as well.

So this one is going to be a two-piece. First of all, following up on the company's most recent results and doing a preview for the year, alongside a valuation prospect for why I believe the company represents an attractive investment. Secondly, why is the company resistant to AI? With these three things cleared up, I believe my targets and expectations both for the long and short term should be clear.

Given that the company reported 2Q25 in August and is set to release earnings in about 4 weeks, I'm also including an earnings preview.

You can find my last article on Merck KGaA here.

Merck KGaA - Upside from low valuation and solid operations

As usual, I will point out first that Merck KGaA is not Merck (MRK). They are two different companies. If you want Merck, you'll have to buy MRK - more about that in the valuation section.

The German Merck KGaA (hereafter referred to as 'Merck' in this article, except where noted) is a life science company. It works, unlike its NA namesake, both in healthcare, life science, and electronics. This focus is something that has been sharpened step-by-step since the post-GFC period, when the company started M&A'ing to get further exposure to these end markets. The results of investors have been superb - if they knew when to exit an overvalued investment. Because I didn't buy it cheaply, I was not "in it" when it peaked in 2021, and thus I have been waiting for the company's price to drop cheaply.

For about a year now, I have believed firmly that Merck is cheap.

The company's expertise lies in products and services related to biopharma. It doesn't really sell anything of note to consumers, but rather to other pharma and biotech companies. Its services and products are used for the manufacture of drugs through the various stages of development. But it's not a "Machinery" or engineering business - its portfolio is mostly products and services that are used continually, providing recurring rather than one-time.

Core challenges for the company were related to the first massive stocking during pandemic periods (the reason for its significant inflation in valuation, if you ask me), as well as the resulting destocking, which has resulted in turn in the downcycle for the company and for the earnings that we've seen since. This is primarily for the life science segment. The healthcare segment, meanwhile, has a drug portfolio focused on biologics. What are biologics?

They are treatments or drugs derived from living organisms, such as various bacteria, plants, animal cells, or things like yeast. "Natural" derivations, if you will. Why is this a good thing?

Because biologics are very hard to replicate - they are molecules produced not in a lab, in this sense, but rather in animals themselves. The opposite of this are simple chemical drugs (simple in the relative sense). Biologics tend to produce variations in the final products (naturally, if you think about it). It means that the final product is subject to variations.

To be clear, I'm not a pharmacist or a doctor - but I've done enough research to understand the competitive edge that this may have.

The last segment for Merck is Electronics. Merck is a key supplier to parts of the semiconductor space, thanks to a diverse portfolio inorganically added through companies like Versum. Not only is Merck therefore "anti-AI" (in terms of susceptibility) for its non-electronics segments, but it also enjoys benefits from the AI trend because of the rising demand for chips for AI. The company's specific manufacturing segment is thin-film solutions.

These are the segments. As you can see, in every way, I believe Merck is positioned to see significant growth from these macro trends - and the rising amount of R&D both from pharma companies as well as from AI companies is adding to the company's sales as well as earnings here. It's a "double advantage". The company's use of JV's and partnerships in its more legacy divisions, like healthcare, also helps it to offset volatility from this segment. I believe, going forward, you'll be hard-pressed to find a better AI-resistant play than this.

Why are pharma companies relatively low-impact in terms of AI? While AI can make these companies more efficient through things like efficiency (recruiting patients for trials, analyzing biological data, automating non-complex tasks) and reduce costs, allowing it to replace the human touch in a way that might be possible in customer service, automated driving, or other things, would open companies up to significant legislative risks.

The combination of these risks, combined with the upside from actual AI revenues, makes Merck an interesting play here.

Risks for Merck are limited to things like legacy portfolio issues - patent expirations, advancements in biologics from other companies (a very general risk), and increasing competition for its more technical segments. Also, there's the obvious political aspect of the pressure to lower drug prices. If there comes such a scheme comes, this will obviously impact earnings.

Recent results from 2Q25 confirm a recovery to growth. The company's organic sales were up 2%, EBITDA over 4.5%, with top-line sales increase in the double digits. US Policy changes are impacting results, as expected in life science, but sales increases in healthcare are driving things up, with solid sales in drugs like Erbitux and Mavenclad.

The company's electronics segment was the sad part in 2Q, reporting a top-line decline of 6% - but on a total view, and coupled with the company's superb leverage of 1.3x net to EBITDA, the company was very solid.

Also, much of the pressure was related to FX.

These trends, coupled with the 2025E result expectations that you see above, detail an EPS that's going to continue to decline in fiscal 2025 - at least I consider this very likely. The current range does include a top-line expectation that would result in very slight growth - but given the current trends in things like electronics, which is experiencing current pressure due to FX, tariffs, and macro, I wouldn't expect Merck to manage this. In the current environment for pharma, and for Merck's peers, I can say with clarity that most of the sector is undervalued, for similar reasons. It means that most companies are waiting for clarity and recovery in the broader market. 3Q25 will not change the company's thesis or 2025E one way or another, but may provide a bit more clarity on where in the €8-€8.7/share AEPS the company is going to land.

My forecast for Merck lies at the midpoint, €8.4/share. I forecast continued sales upsides from legacy, coupled with life science upside (continued as well), to deliver a result in the negative on a YoY basis. Once Electronics stabilizes, which I currently expect at the end of the year or thereabouts, I would expect a slow start to recovery in valuation, aided then by EPS growth in 2026-2027E.

That means the valuation is as follows.

Merck KGaA - Valuation is compelling, with an upside even to conservative estimates

Merck has what you'd usually want out of a company like this. It doesn't share the current volatility of the pure-play pharma and drug companies because the majority of its business is no longer exposed to this. It has an A-credit rating, very, very low debt, a yield of about 2%, and it usually hits or beats targets (80%+ of the time on a 1-year basis with a 10% margin of error).

Furthermore, it's recently been significantly pressured to where it is today, trading at 13.7x P/E, with a typical range of 17-19x P/E. I would put the company at the 7-15 year average, which comes to 18x, especially allowing for its sales and exposure to AI trends in terms of sales, while its other sectors are relatively safe from being "replaced" with AI. The only companies I can think of at this time where things are even safer from this are businesses where much of the operational execution is done purely by hand, and with expertise, such as drilling, construction (the specialized kind), plumbing, or businesses that have legal requirements akin to doctors or attorneys/lawyers in front of a court. The fact is, AI is pervasive and coming into every segment, but we can find companies like this that are prepared and also enjoy the upsides of it.

It's the combination that appeals here, and I believe the 20% annualized RoR you can get from Merck is relatively safe here.

You could argue for a lower multiple, but at this price of €115/share, even a 16x P/E results essentially in a 15-16% annualized RoR, which for a company like this is solid.

Arguments against investing here exist, of course, and the same defensiveness against AI could be argued for other biologics and pharma companies (though without the upside this company has for manufacturing, which is part of what "makes" the thesis here).

Peers that are undervalued include not only direct ones like Biogen (BIIB) and Danaher (DHR), which are good but with different risk profiles, but also something like the US-based Merck - I'm talking about the MRK symbol now. While MKGAF offers compelling value and stability, no one can deny that MRK's current valuation is interesting too. It has somewhat less forecast safety, but trades at a lower multiple and to a lower upside multiple than MKGAF. It has a higher yield as well (but also higher debt, significantly).

The upside for MRK here is compelling, even on a conservative basis.

The risk profile is slightly different, more exposure to drug pricing and the like, which makes it, in my mind, more volatile, but at this valuation, I don't believe you can do "much wrong". I'm seriously considering going "back" into Merck as well - I sold my stake when I thought the US dollar was going down towards the latter part of 2024 to protect my FX. As I said in my last article, you could also consider Sanofi (SNY).

So, to conclude - excellent potential in both companies, and I might buy both, but my favorite for the AI perspective is without a doubt Merck KGaA, which I put at a fair value of €175 conservatively, which implies a PT of €135, and we're currently at $115 native. The only difference from my last article is that I no longer think the company is cheap - that would be below €110/share.

For the MKGAF ADR, that's a PT of $156/share.

Risks & Considerations

The company could, of course, continue to see challenges to its growth pipeline and see further issues, as well as further pressure on its AI-reliant semi operations for the near term, which could impact its growth rate. An impacted growth rate in turn would then result in a lower valuation upside, and this could dilute or lower the. returns that I am expecting for the company. This needs to be put into context of where the company is today, however, and I consider it cheap.

Beyond that, I view Merck's fundamentals as appealing enough to be a very solid "BUY" here.

Thesis

  • Merck KGaA is the world's largest still-operating pharma and healthcare company, with a 300+ year history. It has absolutely stellar fundamentals, and what risks do exist are well-covered by the company's long-term upside and expertise here.
  • I say that at a good valuation, the company could be a "must-BUY" for the long term. Merck has now also dropped below €130/share, which, in my latest article, I made clear was the "cheap" target.
  • I view Merck KGaA as being a "BUY" with a fair value target of at least €175/share for the long term, and a price target of €135/share or below. This implies buying the company's German-listed ticker, which I do believe is the way to go here for the company.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this, and a price target of €135 per share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "buy" rating as of October 2025.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best AI-Resistant Idea investment competition, which runs through November 10. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Mattel: Near-Term Risks Outshine The Positive Developments
data.description:
Investment Overview

I give a hold rating for Mattel Inc. (NASDAQ:MAT) as the setup looks mixed, with near-term uncertainties offsetting the positive POS data trends. While POS data remains positive and management continues to secure key licensing renewals, the reliance on needing a very strong Q4 performance and lower visibility due to the shift to domestic shipping leaves little room for error.

Business Description

MAT owns a portfolio of popular toy brands across major categories like Dolls (Barbie is the key brand here); Infant, Toddler, and Preschool (Fisher-Price and Thomas & Friends are key brands here); Vehicles (Hot Wheels); Action Figures; Building Sets; and Games. Aside from selling the physical toy, MAT also leverages these brands to expand into other entertainment offerings like digital gaming and live experiences.

Q3 2025 Earnings Review

MAT's Q3 2025 results came in below consensus expectations, with net sales down 6% y/y as reported (7% in constant currency) to $1.736 billion. The weakness was largely due to order timing shifts from North American retailers. However, the mix within this decline was not universal from a gross billings perspective. The Dolls segment, which is MAT's most important franchise, saw billings fall 11% y/y to $674 million as demand softness persisted; the Infant, Toddler & Preschool category was also down 25% y/y to $262 million. But on the other hand, vehicles saw good demand, with billings up 8% y/y to $626 million, and the so-called Challenger categories (action figures, building sets, games, and other) also saw 11% y/y growth to $404 million. Seen from this angle, the real drag is really the Dolls and Infant, Toddler & Preschool category. Profitability took a big hit. Adj. gross margin contracted 290 bps y/y to 50.2%, which, combined with sales decline, led to adj. EBIT decline of $117 million y/y to $387 million. As a result, EPS came in at $0.89.

Underlying Demand and Strategic Wins Offer Support to Bull Case

The good news is there are positive demand signals that support the bull case for MAT. Firstly, the best leading indicator of demand, Point-Of-Sale [POS] data, grew across every region, including the US. Compare this to the data point that gross billings saw a y/y decline; it suggests that end-consumer demand remained solid even though retailers are delaying their inventory stock-up. This gap between POS and shipments is important because it dismisses the bear case that demand is eroding. From what I'm seeing, it is just retailers being more cautious on their inventory level, and if underlying demand keeps strong, they have to restock at some point, and that will be a major demand catalyst for MAT.

This momentum has also evidently followed through Q4, as management noted; since the start of Q4 2025, POS trends have stayed positive, and retailer orders in the US have "accelerated significantly." This supports my view above that retailers are "forced" to restock in order to meet the potential demand during the holiday season (should demand momentum continue). Not restocking today would mean retailers missing out on one of the most important seasons of the year.

Secondly, MAT has also strengthened its partnership strategy. Per the disclosure, MAT has renewed its multi-year global licensing deal for Disney Princess and Frozen (these are very important franchises for the Dolls portfolio), and on top of that, MAT has also secured global rights for K-Pop Demon Hunters. These wins have a few implications. One is that these brands still see value in partnering with MAT; two, it significantly de-risks MAT's future revenue streams (the narrative of MAT losing the deal with Disney Princess and Frozen is off the table for now); and (3) adds another growth catalyst (K-Pop Demon Hunters) to FY26.

Headwinds And Execution Risks

Now, that is not to say there is no bear case for MAT. I actually think there are enough bearish points that offset the positives I mentioned above. As much as POS data paints a positive picture for the US, the reality is MAT saw a sharp 12% y/y drop in North America net sales. The reason for this is because retailers have shifted from earlier direct import commitments to later domestic shipping orders, which makes sense for them as this allows them to be flexible given the trade policy situation. However, this means more execution risk for MAT. Unlike the past, when MAT had good visibility into demand, what happened in Q3 showed us that MAT now has lower visibility and greater dependence on just-in-time fulfillment.

This is bearish for MAT because it increases the risk of gross margin pressure. As of Q3 2025, MAT's owned inventory grew ~12%, to $827 million, and the reason for this was to accommodate the shift towards domestic shipping and prepare for a strong Q4. While that logic is sound, carrying more inventory at a time when MAT has lower visibility means that, if in any case Q4 demand turned weaker than expected, or retailer restocking underdelivers, MAT would be forced to mark down its goods to clear inventories. For a business that has managed to pull gross margin up from the high 40s to >50% in recent quarters, this markdown dynamic would put a lid on the market's optimism on the margin front.

Drawing back to the categories that are seeing weakness above (Dolls and Infant, Toddler & Preschool), this is really not a good sign. While MAT has some other categories to offset this weakness, remember that these segments are the flagship brands/franchises for MAT. With them falling by double digits, which may indicate poor underlying demand (management disclosed overall POS data, but not individual categories, so there is a chance that these segments' POS data are so weak that retailers give up restocking).

What this all leads to, in my view, is heavier dependency on Q4 execution. The bulls would celebrate that management reaffirmed guidance, but given the weak Q3, it implies that MAT now needs double-digit revenue growth in Q4 to meet the guide. That implies a steep acceleration relative to the performance on a year-to-date basis and effectively sets a very high bar for this last quarter. Not saying that this wouldn't play out the way management expects, but the risk is for high investors. I would think it's quite binary at the moment to call out whether Q4 demand will be strong or bad, especially given all the macro noises about consumer spending powers being weak. If holiday demand or restocking momentum falls short, MAT could very well miss guidance, and that would, very likely, put pressure on the stock price.

Valuation

As I write this earnings update, MAT trades at 10.5x forward PE, which I think is a very fair multiple when compared to its historical range and peers. Historically, MAT trades at 12.5x, and the 2x discount is fair because growth is evidently soft on a year-to-date basis. While POS data is positive, the weakness in core brands and potential gross margin contraction would mean earnings growth may come in weaker.

When compared to peers like Hasbro (HAS) and Spin Master Corp. (OTCPK:SNMSF), which trade at ~15x and 7x forward P/E, MAT trades in the middle at 10.5x. I also think this is fair because HAS has a higher two-year revenue growth outlook of mid-to-high single digits, while SNMSF has a low-single-digit growth outlook. These compare to MAT, which has a low-to-mid-single-digit growth outlook. Compared this way, I think it's right that MAT trades at a discount to HAS and a premium to SNMSF.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for MAT. The near-term story is mixed. On one hand, we have solid POS trends and new licensing wins that offer upside, but on the other hand, execution risk is high given the lower visibility and dependency on Q4 to meet the guidance. At 10.5x forward P/E, valuation appears fair against peers, and I think the multiple will stay rangebound until MAT shows continuous demand recovery or margin expansion.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CPI At Long Last: Inflation Eases In September, Clearing The Way For A Fed Rate Cut
data.description:
The September CPI report came in softer than expected. Headline CPI rose 0.31%, below the 0.4% consensus, bringing the year-over-year rate to 3.013%. The core rate increased 0.227% last month, under the 0.3% forecast, resulting in a similar 3.019% year-over-year rate.

Both headline and core inflation eased compared to August’s numbers (+0.38% and +0.35%, respectively). Stocks and bonds rallied after the sanguine consumer price data. The Fed is now a clear go for a rate cut next week.

Digging into the report, the 3-month annualized change in core CPI is now 3.64%, still well above the Fed’s 2% year-over-year headline PCE target. Owners’ equivalent rent rose just 0.137%, the smallest increase since November 2020. Meanwhile, supercore CPI came in at 0.352%, up from 0.33% in August, with the annual rate now at 3.176%, down from 3.211% previously.

Core goods CPI, key to the tariff narrative, increased by just 0.2%, with new cars up 0.2% and used vehicle prices falling 0.4%. Hot was apparel up 0.7% in September. Core services also posted a tame 0.2% monthly climb, aided by a 0.4% drop in vehicle insurance costs. Airfares supported services CPI, with that category rising 2.7%.

The Now-Dated September CPI Came in Lighter than Expected

Headline and Core CPI Tame

YoY CPI Inflation Holding Near 3%, Rate Cuts on Tap

3-Month Annualized Core CPI Barely Below 4%

US Inflation Using Real-Time Shelter Price Data Near 2%

Stocks and bonds rose after the sanguine September CPI report. The SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY) printed fresh all-time highs in the premarket, while the yield on the benchmark 10-year Treasury note ticked down to 3.97%.

There was strength in small caps, and the VIX dipped under 17. Gold and Bitcoin caught a morning bid, too.

S&P 500 ETF Hits A New Record

Treasury Rates Dipped After The Cool Inflation Numbers

Gold’s Losses Mitigated Post-CPI

VIX Now Under 17

Looking ahead, it’s a busy week on the macro to wrap up what has so far been a bullish month of October. The big story will be two-fold.

First, Wednesday afternoon’s Fed decision (which will almost certainly be a cut) will offer fresh clues on just how dovish the FOMC will be in the current easing cycle. The Fed may also announce the end of its balance sheet runoff.

Second, the Trump-Xi meeting, slated for Thursday, will be crucial for the lingering trade war. As it stands, the market expects the government shutdown to last 45 days, so we may have to wait until November for a re-opening.

Fed Meeting Next Week

Government Shutdown Seen Lasting 45 Days

Big Earnings Week Ahead

The Bottom Line

September CPI was cooler than expected. Stocks and bonds rallied after the inflation numbers hit the tape, indicative of high prospects for more Fed rate cuts. This was dated data, though, and the government shutdown means limited macro information hitting the tape in the next (likely) three weeks.

data.title: Trump Cancels Trade Talks With Canada Even As U.S. Seeks Broad Alliance To Check China's Rare Earth Dominance
data.description:
Overview

The US dollar is trading higher against all the G10 currencies today, but it has not broken out of this week's ranges. The Dollar Index is rising for the fourth session this week and has recovered last week's 0.55% decline. The greenback is more mixed against emerging market currencies. While the JP Morgan Emerging Market Currency Index is up about 0.15% this week, the MSCI gauge is off by about 0.10%.

Equities are mostly firmer today. In the Asia Pacific region, the Nikkei and China's CSI 300 gained more than 1%, but South Korea's Kospi jumped 2.5% and led the region higher this week with a 5.1% gain. President Lee will meet President Trump on October 29 and President Xi on November 1. The South Korean won recovered from six-month lows following verbal intervention by the finance minister. Despite a better preliminary PMI, the euro struggles to find much traction and the Stoxx 600 is nursing a small loss. US index futures are firm. Aside from the Japanese government benchmark 10-year yield, which slipped a basis point, most other major bond yields are firmer. European yields are mostly 2-4 bp higher, with the increase in France leading the region and the 10-year Gilts doing best with its nearly flat performance. The 10-year Treasury yield is slightly firmer to a fraction above 4.0%. Canada's 10-year yield is about 3.5 bp higher at 3.09%. Gold is off 1.6% after rising almost 0.70% yesterday. Today's low is slightly below $4048. It settled around $4356 on Monday. December WTI is in a narrow range (~$61.20-$62.15) at the upper end of yesterday's surge that lifted it to about $62.20.

USD: The Dollar Index reached almost 99.15 in the middle of the week, and it held yesterday. It remains firm today but in about a 10-tick range on either side of 99.00. Last week's high was almost 99.50. DXY consolidated yesterday and also held above Wednesday's low, a little below 98.80. Today is the busiest the US economic calendar has been since the government shut down a little more than three weeks ago. In order to allow cost-of-living adjustments in Social Security and other programs, the September CPI will be reported today. A 0.4% rise in the headline will lift the year-over-year rate for the fifth consecutive month, and at 3.1%, it would be the highest since May 2024. The core rate has also risen for the past four months, but the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey sees it flat last month at 3.1%. Most Fed officials want to look through the uptick in inflation, with many seeing the impact of the tariffs as being temporary. Moreover, the balance of risks suggests the threat of further deterioration of the labor market is greater. The market has a Fed cut nearly fully discounted for next week, and given the blackout period until the meeting, there is little that officials can say to frame it differently. The preliminary October PMI is also due today, and it is expected to be softer. The composite PMI may have fallen for the third consecutive month and the fourth time in five months. Barring a dramatic revision, the final University of Michigan consumer survey is unlikely to have much impact, and the same can be said of Kansas City Fed's service survey.

Euro: The euro recorded an inside day yesterday, and the market seems to lack near-term conviction. With the Fed and ECB meeting next week, the Trump-Xi meeting at the end of the week, the escalation of sanctions on Russia, uncertainty is running high. Even the stronger-than-expected PMI failed to have much impact, and the euro is in a $1.1600-30 range. The preliminary manufacturing PMI rose to 50.0 from 49.8. The services PMI rose to 52.6 from 51.3. It is the highest since August 2024. The composite PMI rose to 52.2 from 51.2. It is a new high for the year, matching last year's high, which is the best since May 2023. Through September, it averaged 50.6 this year compared with 50.3 in the Jan-Sept 2024 period. Stronger German services appear to explain the strength, which lifted its composite to its best level since May 2023 (53.8 vs. 52.0). France's composite PMI fell to 46.8 (from 48.1). It is the lowest since February's 45.1 reading.

CNY: The PBOC has pushed the dollar fix to new lows. It was set at CNY7.0918 yesterday, the lowest since last October. It was set at CNY7.0928 today. A week ago, it was almost CNY7.0950. Nevertheless, against the offshore yuan, the dollar recorded the low so far for the year near CNH7.0850 on September 17, when the Federal Reserve cut the rates. For the third consecutive session, the dollar is in a CNH7.1230-CNH7.1300 range. Trump and Xi are still likely to meet on the sidelines of the APEC meeting at the end of next week. While Trump holds out the possibility of a deal, the latest sanctions on Russia are also an indirect way to discourage Chinese companies buying Russian oil due to the threat of secondary sanctions. The US is also threatening to impose broad software curbs on China, and it is not coincidental that China's recent press release on the broader and tighter critical mineral export controls was not using Microsoft software.

JPY: The jump in oil prices, the rise in US yields, and a growing conviction that the Bank of Japan will stand pat next week took a toll on the yen. The dollar rose to JPY152.80 yesterday and traded a little above JPY153.00 today. The greenback reached an eight-month high slightly above JPY153.25 on October 10. The US 10-year yield settled fractionally above 4.0% yesterday, and the little more than five basis point increase was the largest since September 17, when the Federal Reserve cut rates. This may have also supported the greenback. Japan reported the November CPI earlier today. As hinted by the Tokyo report out late last month, the headline and core rates rose to 2.9% from 2.7%, while the measure that excludes fresh food and energy eased to 3.0% from 3.3% and is the lowest print since April. Separately, the preliminary October PMI showed the manufacturing contraction continued (48.3 vs. 48.5), while the service PMI continued to expand but slower (52.4 vs. 53.3). The composite PMI slipped to 50.9 from 51.3. The 51.6 average in Q3 was the highest quarterly average since Q3 24. A week ago, the swaps market had a little more than a 25% chance of a BOJ hike next week discounted. It has been more than halved this week.

GBP: Sterling recorded an inside day yesterday and slipped a few hundredths of a cent below Wednesday's low (~$1.3305) but held above $1.33. It has not been above $1.3335 today. Unless it recovers further today, it will be the fifth consecutive session of lower highs. The swaps market is discounting a year-end base rate of about 3.80%, which is off 6-7 basis points this week to around a two-month low. The current target is 4.0%. Today's data points were stronger than expected, and still sterling struggles. The UK reported a 0.5% increase in September retail sales. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey anticipated a 0.4% pullback after a 0.5% gain in August (which was revised to 0.6%).
